words: LENA JUSTICE: On Saturday, why, after we got everything done at home in our
apartment in Deary, why, we decided to walk home and the railroad track
was really the shortest way for us to go. We didn't want to go clear around
the road. So and I, we stuck out. And we got to Hog Meadows, and that
was about a mile and a quarter from our place. Mr. T.P.Jones picked us up
and gave us a lift. The snow was clear up to our knees or better, I guess.
And the folks hadn't broked the road yet, so it was deep and we were so
wet and that was about ten o'clock at night that we got home. That was
miserable. So the next day we walked back, it was on a Sunday, get back
to school again. I can name about the months I had gone to school. It
wasn't very much. Got to the fifth grade, anyway. But May, she went through,
I think May finished high school. She'd work awhile. I think she was
smarter than I was She could sure skip through.(laughs) But anyway, she
was...
LAURA SCHRAGER: Was that many years that you went into Deary?

speaker: LJ
words: No, the first I'd go to school at Helmer. I'd ride a horse, you know, in

speaker: LJ
words: the fall, when you could go up until the snow fly. And then I'd be home

speaker: LJ
words: maybe all winter. Then maybe in the spring I 'd go until school was out.

speaker: LJ
words: But I'd go to Deary and you could always work for your board and room. Go

speaker: LJ
words: to school. And that was hard. 'Cause you had to do youn dishes in the

speaker: LJ
words: morning, you had to get breakfast. And then come back, help with the

speaker: LJ
words: lunch. In the evening, why you had the lunch dishes to wash. This lady

speaker: LJ
words: was kind of lazy, I think. She'd always leave it to me, anyway. I'd wash

speaker: LJ
words: the dishes and start supper. By the time I'd done all that and studied a

speaker: LJ
words: little bit, why, I was all in. Clean house on Saturdays and Sundays.

speaker: LJ
words: She was an old schoolteacher. I think she went to school affier that and

speaker: LJ
words: got the job teaching school. But it was rough, I think I stayed three

speaker: LJ
words: different places, three different winters. There was one place that I

speaker: LJ
words: really enjoyed, and that was Mrs. Wesby. I was just like one of the

speaker: LJ
words: family there. They just, you know. She'd do a lot of work and if I

speaker: LJ
words: wanted to do this why, it was alright. Help her with the dishes, but I

speaker: LJ
words: never had to do anything alone like I did at the other place. And then

speaker: LJ
words: from there, I came to Spokane. Stayed with ft cousin and went to school.

speaker: LJ
words: For a few months. And then I got a job. At Kemp and Hey . Running the

speaker: LJ
words: elevator.

speaker: LJ
words: Kemp and Hdberds in Spokane. They used to be a store by Kemp and Heberds.

speaker: LJ
words: And I worked about let see, four months, I guess. And I thought I was

speaker: LJ
words: pretty rich, I was getting thirteen twenty a week. I can remember that

speaker: LJ
words: check. The money would be cash and they'd put it in an envelop and every

speaker: LJ
words: week, I'd pick up that, and I thought,"Oh boy, that's a lot of money."

speaker: LJ
words: Then I had to go home.

speaker: LS
words: Would you send a lot of it home?

speaker: LJ
words: Oh yes, I'd send some. Not much, I tried t spend it having a good time

speaker: LJ
words: going down to the park every Sunday.(chuckles) .other cousin and I. I

speaker: LJ
words: went home and they thought I was, my brother said,"Well Gee, you haven't

speaker: LJ
words: got any tan. You won't be that way very long when you get out in the hay

speaker: LJ
words: field."I was gonna go to work in the hay field,

speaker: LS
words: You said you were quite a tomboy. What kinds of things would you do?

speaker: LJ
words: Ride horses. I'd like to been in the show, I guess. I'd stand up and I'd

speaker: LJ
words: turn around and ride backwards and ride way up on their neck.I'd rather

speaker: LJ
words: work outside than inside. Try to walk tightrope too, My brother always done

speaker: LJ
words: that. Wasn't as good at it, though. Doing a little acrobatic stuff.

speaker: LS
words: Would you go to Bovill to chataquas that they had?

speaker: LJ
words: Yeah, I did. Then they had moving picture shows there, In the old

speaker: LJ
words: opera house. And they had dances upstairs. It was, they used to have quite

speaker: LJ
words: a lively town there. Years ago. They had ballgames, good ball players.

speaker: LS
words: Do you remember any of those things well enough to give a description of

speaker: LS
words: them? Did you go to a lot of dances?

speaker: LJ
words: No, I didn't go toA many dances because we didn't have the clothes to wear.

speaker: LJ
words: Mostly overalls. I did go to dances there alright, a few times. And the

speaker: LJ
words: shows. But we didn't have the money. And...

speaker: LS
words: Would you go to town much when you were young?

speaker: LJ
words: Oh yeah, we'd go maybe once every two weeks or maybe sometimes once a week.

speaker: LJ
words: It all depend. Mother had a lot of chickens. And we'd take eggs up there

speaker: LJ
words: and trade 'em for sugar or coffee or whatever we needed, you know. Sometimes

speaker: LJ
words: flour. We always could get rid of the eggs. We didn't sell much butter

speaker: LJ
words: though. That was kind of out of the question. But eggs and stuff like tht

speaker: LJ
words: was the main thing. And,we always had a nice big garden, so that helped.

speaker: LS
words: Did you have trouble with frost?

speaker: LJ
words: Oh yeah. We used to have a lot of frost up there.Be early in the fall and

speaker: LJ
words: we'd get it, it'd be late in the spring too, before you could plant anything,

speaker: LJ
words: you know. We have to plant like carrots and stuff like that that would stand

speaker: LJ
words: the cold weather. and rutabagas and beets. Peas, you could plant

speaker: LJ
words: peas, they'd get ripe. But you couldn't plant corn or beans or anything

speaker: LJ
words: like that.

speaker: LS
words: Those meadows get a lot of frost.

speaker: LJ
words: Yeah, there was quite oh the frost would come early and seems as though,

speaker: LJ
words: course we re getting here^in Spokane, seems like in the last few

speaker: LJ
words: years. Mr. Pope was saying yesterday, last year we had frost the first

speaker: LJ
words: of September I think he said. I don't remember it. And he's 93 years old.

speaker: LJ
words: (chuckles)Hope my memory's like that when I,if I live to be that old.

speaker: LS
words: Sounds like you had to grow a lot of the same kind of...

speaker: LJ
words: Yeah, grow a lot of the same kinds of carrots and stuff like that.

speaker: LS
words: Is that what you'd end up eating the year round?

speaker: LJ
words: Well usually, like our potatoes and stuff like that, we could raise nice

speaker: LJ
words: potatoes and we always had enough til the next year, til the other crop

speaker: LJ
words: come on again.Course we'd use those potatoes for seed. But no, in the fall

speaker: LJ
words: of the year we'd go out on the ridges and pick up apples and squashes

speaker: LJ
words: That and some pumpkins, stuff like that, you know couldn't raise.

speaker: LS
words: You'd trade for them?

speaker: LJ
words: Yeah, sometime. And then one time, that was during First World War, Emil,

speaker: LJ
words: that's a brother, he was ill and he couldn't go so we fed a couple of pigs,

speaker: LJ
words: I think there was four of 'em for a guy on the ridge. What was his name

speaker: LJ
words: now? Jim Holt. And we said if we fed 'em through the summer we could have

speaker: LJ
words: two and he would take two. So we had to deliver those pigs. And he said

speaker: LJ
words: if we came down there, we could get a truckload, a wagon load of apples and

speaker: LJ
words: pumpkins and whatever he had to give. So May and I we thought we could

speaker: LJ
words: make it. So we had to hitch up the horses and started out. We got down

speaker: LJ
words: there, took all day to get down there. And you know what the Kendrick grade

speaker: LJ
words: is like. Steep. It's good grade now, compared to what it was then. It was

speaker: LJ
words: just winding roads. So we got down there and I think we stayed a couple days.

speaker: LJ
words: Let the horses rest. And they loaded us up the next day and we had such

speaker: LJ
words: a heavy load on there. We had to block the wagon every time they wanted to

speaker: LJ
words: rest. It was so steep, you know. You go around a curve and let 'em rest and

speaker: LJ
words: then we'd pull 'em on again and then we'd let 'em rest. And iwe'd have to

speaker: LJ
words: put a block behind. So when we got halfway up the hill, the^harnass broke,

speaker: LJ
words: or the tug, I guess it was. And I had to get haywire to wire it up.And

speaker: LJ
words: the poor horses, they were so tired and we were tired. And before we

speaker: LJ
words: got to Deary we stopped at some people there. We thought, maybe we could

speaker: LJ
words: stay overnight here. And it was muddy. The roads, it had rained. Slippery

speaker: LJ
words: and everything. Anyway, we stopped there and asked this lady if we could

speaker: LJ
words: stay overnight. She said."No!"I never will forget that. I thought that

speaker: LJ
words: was awful mean of her. 'Cause we were just young kids, kind of. So we went

speaker: LJ
words: on to the Wesby's and that was about a mile and the horses was

speaker: LJ
words: all in. Course, the only thing they had to feed the horses was straw but

speaker: LJ
words: they took us in and fed us and fed the horses. So the next day we went

speaker: LJ
words: home, and that was eight miles more. And the roads were terrible. Boy were

speaker: LJ
words: we ever glad when we got on the old farm. We had too much stuff on that

speaker: LJ
words: wagon. I don't how many sacks of apples we had. All that. I think it was

speaker: LJ
words: that fall we lost one of the horses. I don't remember now. Memory's getting

speaker: LJ
words: shorter and shorter.

speaker: LS
words: How did you lose it?

speaker: LJ
words: It just got sick. Well, it was apretty old horse too, Isuppose.„ Lived

speaker: LJ
words: it's life. And then I think it was that winter when we had such a hard winter

speaker: LJ
words: and we had to haul hay on a little sled. Horses couldn't make it. My gosh,

speaker: LJ
words: it was awful. That's too sad to talk about that.

speaker: LS
words: Did you haul by hand?

speaker: LJ
words: We had to haul from Meadow because it was water, it just began to

speaker: LJ
words: break in the spring, you know. You know how the snow is crust on top and

speaker: LJ
words: then underneath be water and slush and the horses would fall in here and

speaker: LJ
words: there, jumping. So we'd haul on a little sled, a couple of bales or so.

speaker: LJ
words: In the morning, early morning, when it was really good crust on it, you'd

speaker: LJ
words: haul it for to feed the stock. That was during the First World War when

speaker: LJ
words: my brother Alfred, he was away then. But Emil, he had to take over then.

speaker: LJ
words: And of course, my dad wasn't home then. He was gone.

speaker: LS
words: When you finished school, did you start working?

speaker: LJ
words: Yeah, we started and then we after that, why, we got into logging camps,

speaker: LJ
words: see. On the Shea Meadows and,from there...

speaker: LS
words: Why don't you tell me about what it was like to cook for that, what your

speaker: LS
words: job was?

speaker: LJ
words: well, it was kind of a hard job. We didn't have much to work with, that was

speaker: LJ
words: the worst of it. The camp was new and they had old tables, old bunk tables,

speaker: LJ
words: I don't know what you'd call 'em. Long tables with benches. And seemed like

speaker: LJ
words: it wasAhard to cook and just seemed like it was a little old stove that

speaker: LJ
words: they had in there. It was a Lange, but it was a small stove for the crew.

speaker: LJ
words: And I know we had a lot of milk at home at that time, so we, when I'd go

speaker: LJ
words: home, I'd bring a lot of sour milk so we'd make sour milk hotcakes in the

speaker: LJ
words: mornings. Hot cakes with bacon and eggs and hash brown potatoes. Toast and

speaker: LJ
words: all kinds of cereal. They had to have everything, you know, the lumberjacks.

speaker: LJ
words: But it was kind of funny too, you know the boss.(break in tape) May and I

speaker: LJ
words: we got acquainted with a couple of, well, they were nice young fellas. They

speaker: LJ
words: wanted to take us to a show in Bovill and May and I we thought, well that's

speaker: LJ
words: alright, we can go to the show. And when we came back that night, the

speaker: LJ
words: boss was kind of behind us. He had 3. car, they had a pretty nice little

speaker: LJ
words: car to drive in. I think they had a Ford or something. And this guy, he

speaker: LJ
words: had a pretty nice car driving and he'd sneak up behind and we got through

speaker: LJ
words: the gate. There was a barbwire fence and then they had a gate there. When

speaker: LJ
words: we went through, one of the boys closed the gate. And this other guy, he

speaker: LJ
words: didn t bother open the gate to go. This boss, he went on through

speaker: LJ
words: and tore the gate all to the dickens. And the next morning he said,"Youse

speaker: LJ
words: girls, you're not going to be chasing around. Or we're going to let you

speaker: LJ
words: go." And he told my brother about it too. He said,"I don't want the girls

speaker: LJ
words: to be going out with any boys." We thought, well it was none of his business.

speaker: LJ
words: We was doing our work. If we wanted to go out, we'd go out. So about, must

speaker: LJ
words: have been about a week after that, why, the boss got his walking shoes. He

speaker: LJ
words: was so funny. He got canned. The superintendent, he came up there and canned

speaker: LJ
words: him. He was drinking on the job. He like his liquor pretty well.So after

speaker: LJ
words: that, I guess it was about a week or two after he got canned, why.they

speaker: LJ
words: laid us off. The cook and May and I. And they put in, they made a bigger

speaker: LJ
words: camp out of it and they put in men flunkeys and cooks. A cook. I can't

speaker: LJ
words: remember what year. Anyway, we was home for a while. I guess it was the

speaker: LJ
words: next year when the superintendent came over and he said,"Would you girls

speaker: LJ
words: like to go to work?" And we said yes.

speaker: LS
words: Was this T.P. Jones?

speaker: LJ
words: No. T.P. Jones was out of it then.Mr. Nogle. He was the superintendent of

speaker: LJ
words: the woods then. So he came over.

speaker: LS
words: He came out first.

speaker: LJ
words: Yeah, he did. He rode a horse and buggy. Or did he have his car then?

speaker: LJ
words: I think he had the horse and buggy and then he took us to Bovill and then

speaker: LJ
words: we took the car. Now, I might be wrong. But anyway. So we said, yes, wed

speaker: LJ
words: like to go to work. Mother didn't like to have us go, but, so we got our

speaker: LJ
words: clothes, what little duds we had. High topped shoes and all. (laughs)We

speaker: LJ
words: went down to Princeton. Down to Camp one. down there and they had a

speaker: LJ
words: big, fat German cook. His name was Herman. So the next morning we went to

speaker: LJ
words: work for this guy. We stayed with the boss' family until they got a shack

speaker: LJ
words: fixed for us. One of those marion shacks with two rooms in it. Course they

speaker: LJ
words: had to wash it out. They steamed it out with the engine some way. Put

speaker: LJ
words: the hose in there and got all the bugs out of it, I guess. Wasn't any

speaker: LJ
words: bugs when we moved in anyway. Bedbugs. So anyhow, the girl that was

speaker: LJ
words: working there, maybe I shouldn't tell this. If she ever had it read she'd

speaker: LJ
words: know it was her. Anyway, we kind of took her place.

speaker: LS
words: What was your job there?

speaker: LJ
words: Waiting tables, peeling potatoes scrubbing the floors, washing the dishes

speaker: LJ
words: just about everything we had to do. Frosting the cakes for the cook. He

speaker: LJ
words: was a great big fat cook. German. "My little ones." he said. Always,

speaker: LJ
words: "My little ones." So that went on, how long did we work down there?

speaker: LJ
words: Several months, anyway. Anyway, this cook, he was giving away stuff to some

speaker: LJ
words: friends up there. Some other German lived up there. And they caught him,

speaker: LJ
words: I guess. Well in the meantime they got a^cook that didn't want women

speaker: LJ
words: flunkies. So then they said,"Would you like to go up to Shea Meadows?"

speaker: LJ
words: That's where we first started. They built that camp up real big then. They

speaker: LJ
words: had the horses and barns and bunkhouses and everything there. So of course,

speaker: LJ
words: we couldn't say no. We wanted the job and there was only about a lile and

speaker: LJ
words: a half from our place and it was nice place. There's where I met my

speaker: LJ
words: s husband.

speaker: LS
words: He was the cook there?

speaker: LJ
words: He was the cook there. He and his brother. So they said they needed a

speaker: LJ
words: couple of flunkeys up there, so the strawboss, I guess you'd call him,

speaker: LJ
words: the assistant superintendent.Walt Pierce he met us at the train at Helmer

speaker: LJ
words: and took us on up the camp. There's where we met Charlie, his brother.

speaker: LJ
words: We worked there. And that was nice there, we could go home every once in

speaker: LJ
words: awhile. It was only, in the evenings when we got through with our work,

speaker: LJ
words: why we'd walk home. See how everybody was and same in the wintertime. We

speaker: LJ
words: worked there a year or two.And then the...

speaker: LS
words: Is there any difference between the way different cooks cooked?

speaker: LJ
words: Oh yes, there is a lot of difference. Some cooks is sloppy cooks just

speaker: LJ
words: like this one that was down at, that German cook who was darn many flies

speaker: LJ
words: in that place down there at Princeton, that was Camp One. And he had

speaker: LJ
words: raisins in his cupcakes. YOu didn't know if it was flies or raisins.(laughs)

speaker: LJ
words: He'd be stirring away, and the cook usually used ft hand them days. They

speaker: LJ
words: didn't have any machines to stir with. Stir- away there,

speaker: LJ
words: cupcakes. He had nice He had beautiful cakes.

speaker: LJ
words: Fine grain and all from scratch, not like they have nowadays. Al was a

speaker: LJ
words: good cook too. He was one of the best cooks.What he's got a name for,

speaker: LJ
words: one of the best cooks the Potlatch ever had. So ...

speaker: LS
words: Did he have a reputation for anything in particular?

speaker: LJ
words: Well his cooking was more like home cooking. I mean it was,I don't know.

speaker: LJ
words: His food was good and clean.He always had, whatever he prepared was always

speaker: LJ
words: good.He could take a tough piece of steak and he could make a tender steak

speaker: LJ
words: out of it. He cut his own meat and everything. Some of those cooks didn't

speaker: LJ
words: care how they cut it. But old Herman Byers, he was a pretty good cook

speaker: LJ
words: alright, but he was a sloppy cook. You know, some are sloppy, they're kind

speaker: LJ
words: of dirty cooks. And he was awful fussy. Anybody talking in the dining

speaker: LJ
words: room, why he had a gun on top of the shelf where he had his dishes. And

speaker: LJ
words: he Kept it anybody get smart, I don't know if he'd use it or not. But up there

speaker: LJ
words: anyway. I didn't tell you about, I should tell you about when

speaker: LJ
words: we were working down at Princeton, whey we'd go out. Go to dances, you

speaker: LJ
words: know. And we'd sneak out because the cook didn't want us, he was another

speaker: LJ
words: one, you know that didn't want us -o go out at all. So the bullcook, he's

speaker: LJ
words: the guy that comes and builds fires, carries the wood and all that, water,

speaker: LJ
words: he'd come in to our, well, we'd call it the shack. He usually comes in there

speaker: LJ
words: in the morning and build a roaring fire. You could see the fire come clear

speaker: LJ
words: out of the chimney. Anyway, this particular, I think it was a Saturday

speaker: LJ
words: night, we went to ft dance down at Kennedy Ford, that's pretty close to

speaker: LJ
words: Palouse. And we got home about three o'clock in the morning. And so May

speaker: LJ
words: and I come in to work and the bullcook says,"Well girls, did you have a

speaker: LJ
words: good time last night?" And the cook heard it. Whew! Was he mad!(laughs)

speaker: LJ
words: "He said,"You little ones. Did you go to the dance last night?"We said yes.

speaker: LJ
words: And he wouldn't talk to us. He was pouting.So he was mad and he had his bunk

speaker: LJ
words: right along side our dining room there. So when he took his afternoon off

speaker: LJ
words: May and I finished up, we thought, that old guy. We're going to have some

speaker: LJ
words: fun. So we set the alarm clock and put it by his door and went over

speaker: LJ
words: to our shack and let it ring. Kind of mean.(laughs) He was mad and he wouldn't

speaker: LJ
words: even talk to us!(laughs) Made him madder yet. He thought he owned me, I

speaker: LJ
words: guess.

speaker: LS
words: Is that why the cooks didn't want women flunkeys? The figured they'd fool

speaker: LS
words: around with the men?

speaker: LJ
words: I think so. Or the cooks would get jealous. That's qute a few didn't want

speaker: LJ
words: women flunkeys. There was another guy up, his name was Burt Clarke. He,

speaker: LJ
words: some of them wanted women flunkeys 'cause they said the women was cleaner.

speaker: LJ
words: But some didn't. Course Albert, he said he'd rather have women flunkeys.

speaker: LJ
words: He said theyfd keep things up much better than the men. Scrubbing floors

speaker: LJ
words: and all your dishes and things like that.

speaker: LS
words: How often did you scrub the floors?

speaker: LJ
words: once a week. With a broom. Pour the water on it. Brush it out the

speaker: LJ
words: door. And just as white as could be. Use lot of soap and maybe some lye

speaker: LJ
words: in it too. Albert, he wa inted me to cut hot bread. He always like to

speaker: LJ
words: serve hot bread out of the , right out of the oven practically. You know

speaker: LJ
words: how hard that is to cut. You always had good sharp knives. And I said,

speaker: LJ
words: I'm not going to cut that bread. Too darn hard. He got mad and he come

speaker: LJ
words: over there and he just sliced that bread. We had hot bread on the table.

speaker: LJ
words: That's before I married him, of course, (laughs) That was a different

speaker: LJ
words: song then. I got to be boss.

speaker: LS
words: Did you keep working after you got married?

speaker: LJ
words: Yeah, I worked for a while. And then a year, two months, three months

speaker: LJ
words: Betty was born so I worked off and on. To relieve second cooks and all

speaker: LJ
words: like that.

speaker: LS
words: To give them some free time?

speaker: LJ
words: YEah.

speaker: LJ
words: (End of side A)

speaker: LJ
words: ... got out logs, huge logs that they made those bunkhouses and kitchen

speaker: LJ
words: and all that that they had. In days they just had men cooks and

speaker: LJ
words: flunkeys. I think my brother flunkied for a while. I can't remember if

speaker: LJ
words: it was there or another camp. I believe it was Moose Creek. And they

speaker: LJ
words: used to have separate tables for the Italians. Because they shipped them

speaker: LJ
words: in from Italy. They said, they'd eat, butter and all that with their fingers

speaker: LJ
words: I guess they didn't use their knives. I don't know. I suppose didn't

speaker: LJ
words: know how to eat or something. They had a bunch of those. I don't know.

speaker: LJ
words: Other nationality. But they had separate tables for them. They used to

speaker: LJ
words: talk about, I can't think of it. Snipe hunting. At night.(chuckles)

speaker: LS
words: What was that ?

speaker: LJ
words: Silly. They'd take a guy out and, out in the woods, that's the fun they' d

speaker: LJ
words: have in the logging camps years ago. Say,"Well we got to go snipe hunting.

speaker: LJ
words: You take the lantern and hold this gunny sack. Then we'll go round and

speaker: LJ
words: chase this into the sack see."

speaker: LS
words: What's a snipe?

speaker: LJ
words: Supposed to be a bird, I guess, (laughs)So the guy'd be standing there

speaker: LJ
words: all night. Holding the lantern and holding the sack.They guys'd go home

speaker: LJ
words: and go to bed.(laughs)That's snipe hunting.

speaker: LS
words: Who would fall for that?

speaker: LJ
words: those green horns that would come to camp. Just like used to

speaker: LJ
